question:
A-5. Let \( u(t) \) be a continuous function in the system of differential equations
\[
\frac{d x}{d t}=-2 y+u(t), \quad \frac{d y}{d t}=-2 x+u(t)
\]

Show that, regardless of the choice of \( u(t) \), the solution of the system which satisfies \( x=x_{0}, y=y_{0} \) at \( t=0 \) will never pass through \( (0,0) \) unless \( x_{0}=y_{0} \). When \( x_{0}=y_{0} \), show that, for any positive value \( t_{0} \) of \( t \), it is possible to choose \( u(t) \) so the solution is at \( (0,0) \) when \( t=t_{0} \).
solution:
A-5 Subtracting the two equations eliminates \( u(t) \) and provides the simpler equation \( d(x-y) / d t=2(x-y) \), which has the solution \( x-y=\left(x_{0}-y_{0}\right) e^{2 t} \). If \( x_{0} \neq y_{0} \), the right hand side is never zero and so \( x=y=0 \) can never occur.

For the second part, \( x_{0}=y_{0} \). In this case, \( x(t)=y(t) \) and so every solution is a parametrization of the line \( x=y \). We can attempt to get a solution of the form \( x=x_{0}-a t, y=y_{0}-a t \). This will be a solution if \( u(t)=2\left(x_{0}-a t\right)-a \). By taking \( a=x_{0} / t_{0}, x=y=0 \) at \( t=t_{0} \).

descriptive_long.question:
A-5. Let \( controlf(timevar) \) be a continuous function in the system of differential equations
\[
\frac{d positionx}{d timevar}=-2 positiony+controlf(timevar), \quad \frac{d positiony}{d timevar}=-2 positionx+controlf(timevar)
\]

Show that, regardless of the choice of \( controlf(timevar) \), the solution of the system which satisfies \( positionx=initialx, positiony=initialy \) at \( timevar=0 \) will never pass through \( (0,0) \) unless \( initialx=initialy \). When \( initialx=initialy \), show that, for any positive value \( targett \) of \( timevar \), it is possible to choose \( controlf(timevar) \) so the solution is at \( (0,0) \) when \( timevar=targett \).
descriptive_long.solution:
A-5 Subtracting the two equations eliminates \( controlf(timevar) \) and provides the simpler equation \( d(positionx-positiony) / d timevar=2(positionx-positiony) \), which has the solution \( positionx-positiony=\left(initialx-initialy\right) e^{2 timevar} \). If \( initialx \neq initialy \), the right hand side is never zero and so \( positionx=positiony=0 \) can never occur.

For the second part, \( initialx=initialy \). In this case, \( positionx(timevar)=positiony(timevar) \) and so every solution is a parametrization of the line \( positionx=positiony \). We can attempt to get a solution of the form \( positionx=initialx-slopepar timevar, positiony=initialy-slopepar timevar \). This will be a solution if \( controlf(timevar)=2\left(initialx-slopepar timevar\right)-slopepar \). By taking \( slopepar=initialx / targett, positionx=positiony=0 \) at \( timevar=targett \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
A-5. Let \( velvetquill(canyontrail) \) be a continuous function in the system of differential equations
\[
\frac{d orchidpetal}{d canyontrail}=-2 marblegrain+velvetquill(canyontrail), \quad \frac{d marblegrain}{d canyontrail}=-2 orchidpetal+velvetquill(canyontrail)
\]

Show that, regardless of the choice of \( velvetquill(canyontrail) \), the solution of the system which satisfies \( orchidpetal=orchidseed, marblegrain=marbleseed \) at \( canyontrail=0 \) will never pass through \( (0,0) \) unless \( orchidseed=marbleseed \). When \( orchidseed=marbleseed \), show that, for any positive value \( canyoncrest \) of \( canyontrail \), it is possible to choose \( velvetquill(canyontrail) \) so the solution is at \( (0,0) \) when \( canyontrail=canyoncrest \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-5 Subtracting the two equations eliminates \( velvetquill(canyontrail) \) and provides the simpler equation \( d(orchidpetal-marblegrain) / d canyontrail=2(orchidpetal-marblegrain) \), which has the solution \( orchidpetal-marblegrain=\left(orchidseed-marbleseed\right) e^{2 canyontrail} \). If \( orchidseed \neq marbleseed \), the right hand side is never zero and so \( orchidpetal=marblegrain=0 \) can never occur.

For the second part, \( orchidseed=marbleseed \). In this case, \( orchidpetal(canyontrail)=marblegrain(canyontrail) \) and so every solution is a parametrization of the line \( orchidpetal=marblegrain \). We can attempt to get a solution of the form \( orchidpetal=orchidseed-lanternbeam \\, canyontrail, marblegrain=marbleseed-lanternbeam \\, canyontrail \). This will be a solution if \( velvetquill(canyontrail)=2\left(orchidseed-lanternbeam \\, canyontrail\right)-lanternbeam \). By taking \( lanternbeam=orchidseed / canyoncrest, orchidpetal=marblegrain=0 \) at \( canyontrail=canyoncrest \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
A-5. Let \( staticinput(spacevalue) \) be a continuous function in the system of differential equations
\[
\frac{d verticalaxis}{d spacevalue}=-2 horizontalaxis+staticinput(spacevalue), \quad \frac{d horizontalaxis}{d spacevalue}=-2 verticalaxis+staticinput(spacevalue)
\]

Show that, regardless of the choice of \( staticinput(spacevalue) \), the solution of the system which satisfies \( verticalaxis=finalvertical, horizontalaxis=terminalhorizontal \) at \( spacevalue=0 \) will never pass through \((0,0)\) unless \( finalvertical=terminalhorizontal \). When \( finalvertical=terminalhorizontal \), show that, for any positive value \( spacestart \) of \( spacevalue \), it is possible to choose \( staticinput(spacevalue) \) so the solution is at \((0,0)\) when \( spacevalue=spacestart \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-5 Subtracting the two equations eliminates \( staticinput(spacevalue) \) and provides the simpler equation \( d(verticalaxis-horizontalaxis) / d spacevalue = 2(verticalaxis-horizontalaxis) \), which has the solution \( verticalaxis-horizontalaxis=(finalvertical-terminalhorizontal)e^{2 spacevalue} \). If \( finalvertical \neq terminalhorizontal \), the right-hand side is never zero and so \( verticalaxis=horizontalaxis=0 \) can never occur.

For the second part, \( finalvertical=terminalhorizontal \). In this case, \( verticalaxis(spacevalue)=horizontalaxis(spacevalue) \) and so every solution is a parametrization of the line \( verticalaxis=horizontalaxis \). We can attempt to get a solution of the form \( verticalaxis=finalvertical-curvedness\,spacevalue, \; horizontalaxis=terminalhorizontal-curvedness\,spacevalue \). This will be a solution if \( staticinput(spacevalue)=2(finalvertical-curvedness\,spacevalue)-curvedness \). By taking \( curvedness=finalvertical / spacestart, \; verticalaxis=horizontalaxis=0 \) at \( spacevalue=spacestart \).

garbled_string.question:
A-5. Let \( fkdajshg(vmbnclke) \) be a continuous function in the system of differential equations
\[
\frac{d qzxwvtnp}{d vmbnclke}=-2 hjgrksla+fkdajshg(vmbnclke), \quad \frac{d hjgrksla}{d vmbnclke}=-2 qzxwvtnp+fkdajshg(vmbnclke)
\]

Show that, regardless of the choice of \( fkdajshg(vmbnclke) \), the solution of the system which satisfies \( qzxwvtnp=lqnsdyeo, hjgrksla=ztpfrkhd \) at \( vmbnclke=0 \) will never pass through \( (0,0) \) unless \( lqnsdyeo=ztpfrkhd \). When \( lqnsdyeo=ztpfrkhd \), show that, for any positive value \( bsmnqwer \) of \( vmbnclke \), it is possible to choose \( fkdajshg(vmbnclke) \) so the solution is at \( (0,0) \) when \( vmbnclke=bsmnqwer \).
garbled_string.solution:
A-5 Subtracting the two equations eliminates \( fkdajshg(vmbnclke) \) and provides the simpler equation \( d(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla) / d vmbnclke=2(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla) \), which has the solution \( qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla=(lqnsdyeo-ztpfrkhd) e^{2 vmbnclke} \). If \( lqnsdyeo \neq ztpfrkhd \), the right hand side is never zero and so \( qzxwvtnp=hjgrksla=0 \) can never occur.

For the second part, \( lqnsdyeo=ztpfrkhd \). In this case, \( qzxwvtnp(vmbnclke)=hjgrksla(vmbnclke) \) and so every solution is a parametrization of the line \( qzxwvtnp=hjgrksla \). We can attempt to get a solution of the form \( qzxwvtnp=lqnsdyeo-rplxjgmu vmbnclke, hjgrksla=ztpfrkhd-rplxjgmu vmbnclke \). This will be a solution if \( fkdajshg(vmbnclke)=2(lqnsdyeo-rplxjgmu vmbnclke)-rplxjgmu \). By taking \( rplxjgmu=lqnsdyeo / bsmnqwer \), \( qzxwvtnp=hjgrksla=0 \) at \( vmbnclke=bsmnqwer \).

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer n \geq  3, a transfer time t_0 > 0, an energy budget E > 0 and an amplitude budget M > 0.  

Throughout let u:[0,t_0] \to  \mathbb{R} be any Lebesgue-measurable control that satisfies  

 (Amplitude) |u(t)| \leq  M for a.e. t\in [0,t_0],  
 (Energy)  \int _0^{t_0} u(t)^2 dt \leq  E.  

On \mathbb{R}^{2n} consider the block-diagonal controlled system  

 x_i = 4 x_i + u(t),  i = 1,\ldots ,n     (1a)  
 y_i = -3 y_i + u(t),  i = 1,\ldots ,n     (1b)

with initial state X_0 = (x_{10},\ldots ,x_{n0},y_{10},\ldots ,y_{n0}).  
The target set is the two-dimensional affine subspace  

 S = { (\alpha ,\ldots ,\alpha  n , \beta ,\ldots ,\beta  n ) : \alpha ,\beta \in \mathbb{R} }.  

(A) Show that for every control u the (2n-2) functions  

 \Delta _{ij}(t)=e^{-4t}(x_i(t)-x_j(t)), \Gamma _{ij}(t)=e^{3t}(y_i(t)-y_j(t))

are invariants of motion.

(B) Deduce that the trajectory can reach S at some time \tau  > 0 only if  

 x_{10}=\cdots =x_{n0}, y_{10}=\cdots =y_{n0}.       (2)

(C) Assume (2) and denote \alpha _0:=x_{10}=\cdots =x_{n0}, \beta _0:=y_{10}=\cdots =y_{n0}.  
Show that the 2n-dimensional problem reduces to the two-dimensional system  

 \alpha  = 4\alpha +u(t), \beta  = -3\beta +u(t), (\alpha (0),\beta (0))=(\alpha _0,\beta _0).  (3)

Given an arbitrary target (\alpha _1,\beta _1)\in \mathbb{R}^2, prove that all controls steering (3) to (\alpha _1,\beta _1) in the fixed time t_0 form the affine subspace  

 U = { u\in L^2[0,t_0] : T u = h },         (4)

where  

 T:L^2[0,t_0]\to \mathbb{R}^2, Tu=(\int _0^{t_0}e^{-4s}u(s)ds, \int _0^{t_0}e^{3s}u(s)ds),  
 h=(e^{-4t_0}\alpha _1-\alpha _0, e^{3t_0}\beta _1-\beta _0).

(D) Introduce the controllability Gramian W(t_0):=T T*.  

(i) Prove that W(t_0) is positive definite for every t_0 > 0.  

(ii) Show that U contains a unique element u* of minimum L^2-energy, namely  

 u* = T* W(t_0)^{-1} h,  E_min(t_0):=\int _0^{t_0}u*(t)^2dt=\langle h, W(t_0)^{-1}h\rangle .

(iii) Express u* in the closed form  

 u*(t)=a e^{-4t}+b e^{3t}, 0\leq t\leq t_0,

and give a,b explicitly in terms of (t_0,\alpha _0,\alpha _1,\beta _0,\beta _1).

(E) Amplitude analysis.

(i) (A universal necessary bound)  
Prove that every admissible steering control must satisfy  

 M \geq  M_low:=max{4|h_1|/(1-e^{-4t_0}), 3|h_2|/(e^{3t_0}-1)}, h=(h_1,h_2).

(ii) (Amplitude of the minimum-energy control)  
For u*(t)=a e^{-4t}+b e^{3t} show that
  (a) u* is strictly monotone when ab \leq  0;  
  (b) when ab > 0 it has a unique stationary point  

    t_c = (1/7) ln(4a/3b)  

   which is a strict local minimum of u* if a,b > 0 and a strict local maximum of u* if a,b < 0.  

Deduce that in every case  

 M_* := ess sup_{t\in [0,t_0]}|u*(t)| = max{|a+b|, |a e^{-4t_0}+b e^{3t_0}|}.

(iii) (Simultaneous budgets)

 (a) Show that if E \geq  E_min(t_0) and M \geq  M_* then the 2n-system (1) is reachable in time t_0 (sufficiency).  

 (b) Show that if either E < E_min(t_0) or M < M_low the transfer is impossible (necessity).  

 (c) Explain why in general M_low \leq  M_*, with strict inequality possible.  
Give an explicit numerical example (specify n,t_0,\alpha _0,\beta _0,\alpha _1,\beta _1) for which M_low < M_*, and construct a steering control that respects the smaller bound M_low (necessarily of higher energy than u*).

(F) Give an explicit initial condition that violates (2) (e.g. x_{10}=1,x_{20}=0) and use the invariants from (A) to prove that no admissible control can make the trajectory reach S in finite time.
kernel_variant.solution:
(A) Invariance.  
Differentiate \Delta _{ij}(t)=e^{-4t}(x_i-x_j):

 d\Delta _{ij}/dt = e^{-4t}(4x_i+u-4x_j-u)-4e^{-4t}(x_i-x_j)=0.

The same computation with \Gamma _{ij}(t)=e^{3t}(y_i-y_j) gives d\Gamma _{ij}/dt=0.  
Hence the (2n-2) quantities are conserved for every admissible control u.

(B) Condition (2).  
If X(\tau )\in S then x_i(\tau )=x_j(\tau ) and y_i(\tau )=y_j(\tau ); consequently \Delta _{ij}(\tau )=\Gamma _{ij}(\tau )=0.  
Because the invariants are constant, they must already vanish at t=0, which is exactly (2).

(C) Reduction to two dimensions.  
Under (2) set \alpha :=x_1=\cdots =x_n and \beta :=y_1=\cdots =y_n; substituting into (1) yields (3).  
Solving (3) on [0,t_0] and imposing (\alpha (t_0),\beta (t_0))=(\alpha _1,\beta _1) produces

 Tu = (\int _0^{t_0}e^{-4s}u(s)ds, \int _0^{t_0}e^{3s}u(s)ds)=h,

i.e. the affine subspace (4).

(D) Minimum-energy synthesis.

(i) Let c=(c_1,c_2)\neq 0.  If T* c=0, then c_1e^{-4t}+c_2e^{3t}=0 for all t\in [0,t_0], impossible because the two exponentials are linearly independent. Hence T* is injective and W=T T* is positive definite.

(ii) Because Range T is closed, the orthogonal projection of 0 onto U is u*=T*W^{-1}h; its energy is \langle h,W^{-1}h\rangle .

(iii)  Computations:

 w_{11}=\int _0^{t_0}e^{-8s}ds=(1-e^{-8t_0})/8,  
 w_{22}=\int _0^{t_0}e^{6s}ds=(e^{6t_0}-1)/6,  
 w_{12}=w_{21}=\int _0^{t_0}e^{-s}ds=(1-e^{-t_0}).

Let \Delta  := w_{11}w_{22}-w_{12}^2 > 0.  Then

 a=(w_{22}h_1-w_{12}h_2)/\Delta , b=(-w_{12}h_1+w_{11}h_2)/\Delta ,

so u*(t)=a e^{-4t}+b e^{3t}.

(E) Amplitude questions.

(i)  By Holder

 |h_1| \leq  M\int _0^{t_0}e^{-4s}ds = M(1-e^{-4t_0})/4,  
 |h_2| \leq  M\int _0^{t_0}e^{3s}ds  = M(e^{3t_0}-1)/3,

whence M \geq  M_low.

(ii)  Put f(t)=u*(t)=a e^{-4t}+b e^{3t}.  
 * If ab\leq 0, f'(t)=-4a e^{-4t}+3b e^{3t} keeps a constant sign, so |f| is monotone and attains its maximum at one of the endpoints.  
 * If ab>0, f' vanishes only at  

  t_c=(1/7)ln(4a/3b).  

  Because f''(t)=16a e^{-4t}+9b e^{3t}, we obtain  

   f''(t_c)=28a e^{-4t_c}.  

  Hence t_c is a strict local minimum of f when a,b>0 and a strict local maximum of f when a,b<0.  In either case it is a strict local minimum of |f|, so the essential supremum of |u*| is still attained at an endpoint.  

Consequently  

 M_* = max{|f(0)|,|f(t_0)|} = max{|a+b|, |a e^{-4t_0}+b e^{3t_0}|}.

(iii) Simultaneous budgets.

(a)  If E \geq  E_min and M \geq  M_*, the control u* satisfies both constraints, hence (1) is reachable.

(b)  If E<E_min no element of U has enough energy; if M<M_low no element of U can obey the amplitude bound.  Reachability is impossible.

(c)  The inequality M_low \leq  M_* is immediate because u* itself belongs to U.  Strict inequality can occur.

Example with strict inequality and explicit admissible control.  
Take  

 n = 3, t_0 = 1, \alpha _0 = \beta _0 = 0, \alpha _1 = 1, \beta _1 = \beta ,

where \beta  is chosen so that the two terms in M_low coincide:

 4 e^{-4}\alpha _1 /(1-e^{-4}) = 3 e^{3}\beta  /(e^{3}-1).

Solving gives  

 \beta  = \alpha _1\cdot [4 e^{-4}(e^{3}-1)]/[3 e^{3}(1-e^{-4})] \approx  2.364 \times  10^{-2}.

For these data  

 h = (e^{-4}\alpha _1, e^{3}\beta ) \approx  (1.8315\cdot 10^{-2}, 4.740\cdot 10^{-1}),  

 M_low = 4|h_1|/(1-e^{-4}) \approx  7.45 \times  10^{-2}.  

The minimum-energy control computed above yields  

 M_* \approx  1.23 \times  10^{-1} > M_low.

A steering control that respects the smaller bound is the constant control  

 u_const(t) = M_low\cdot sign(\alpha _1) (here positive), 0 \leq  t \leq  t_0.

Indeed

 Tu_const = ( M_low(1-e^{-4})/4 , M_low(e^{3}-1)/3 ) = h,

so the state reaches the target in time t_0 while |u_const(t)| = M_low for all t.  
Its energy E_const = M_low^2 t_0 is strictly larger than E_min, thereby illustrating the requested phenomenon.

(F)  Take X_0=(1,0,0,\ldots ,0,0,\ldots ,0).  Then \Delta _{12}(0)=1, hence \Delta _{12}(t)\equiv 1 and x_1(t)\neq x_2(t) for all t\geq 0.  Therefore the trajectory can never lie in S, regardless of the control.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension: The problem is posed in 2n dimensions (n≥3), instead of 2 or 3.  
2. Multiple constraints: Both an L∞ (amplitude) and an L² (energy) constraint are enforced simultaneously.  
3. Reduction & functional analysis: Reachability is reduced to an infinite–dimensional moment problem; solving it calls for Hilbert–space projection arguments and computation of a controllability Gramian, techniques absent from the original problem.  
4. Optimisation: The solver must identify and compute the minimum–energy control (quadratic optimisation in function space) and then sharpen it to an amplitude–optimal control, mixing optimal–control theory with real analysis.  
5. Necessity & sufficiency: One has to prove precise reachability conditions (parts (B), (E), (F)), not merely supply a single steering control.  
6. Interaction of concepts: Linear invariants, controllability, Gramian inversion, and optimisation under mixed L²/L∞ constraints all interact, greatly deepening the technical load relative to the original question, which used only a single elementary invariant and elementary integration.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer n \geq  3, a transfer time t_0 > 0, an energy budget E > 0 and an amplitude budget M > 0.  

Throughout let u:[0,t_0] \to  \mathbb{R} be any Lebesgue-measurable control that satisfies  

 (Amplitude) |u(t)| \leq  M for a.e. t\in [0,t_0],  
 (Energy)  \int _0^{t_0} u(t)^2 dt \leq  E.  

On \mathbb{R}^{2n} consider the block-diagonal controlled system  

 x_i = 4 x_i + u(t),  i = 1,\ldots ,n     (1a)  
 y_i = -3 y_i + u(t),  i = 1,\ldots ,n     (1b)

with initial state X_0 = (x_{10},\ldots ,x_{n0},y_{10},\ldots ,y_{n0}).  
The target set is the two-dimensional affine subspace  

 S = { (\alpha ,\ldots ,\alpha  n , \beta ,\ldots ,\beta  n ) : \alpha ,\beta \in \mathbb{R} }.  

(A) Show that for every control u the (2n-2) functions  

 \Delta _{ij}(t)=e^{-4t}(x_i(t)-x_j(t)), \Gamma _{ij}(t)=e^{3t}(y_i(t)-y_j(t))

are invariants of motion.

(B) Deduce that the trajectory can reach S at some time \tau  > 0 only if  

 x_{10}=\cdots =x_{n0}, y_{10}=\cdots =y_{n0}.       (2)

(C) Assume (2) and denote \alpha _0:=x_{10}=\cdots =x_{n0}, \beta _0:=y_{10}=\cdots =y_{n0}.  
Show that the 2n-dimensional problem reduces to the two-dimensional system  

 \alpha  = 4\alpha +u(t), \beta  = -3\beta +u(t), (\alpha (0),\beta (0))=(\alpha _0,\beta _0).  (3)

Given an arbitrary target (\alpha _1,\beta _1)\in \mathbb{R}^2, prove that all controls steering (3) to (\alpha _1,\beta _1) in the fixed time t_0 form the affine subspace  

 U = { u\in L^2[0,t_0] : T u = h },         (4)

where  

 T:L^2[0,t_0]\to \mathbb{R}^2, Tu=(\int _0^{t_0}e^{-4s}u(s)ds, \int _0^{t_0}e^{3s}u(s)ds),  
 h=(e^{-4t_0}\alpha _1-\alpha _0, e^{3t_0}\beta _1-\beta _0).

(D) Introduce the controllability Gramian W(t_0):=T T*.  

(i) Prove that W(t_0) is positive definite for every t_0 > 0.  

(ii) Show that U contains a unique element u* of minimum L^2-energy, namely  

 u* = T* W(t_0)^{-1} h,  E_min(t_0):=\int _0^{t_0}u*(t)^2dt=\langle h, W(t_0)^{-1}h\rangle .

(iii) Express u* in the closed form  

 u*(t)=a e^{-4t}+b e^{3t}, 0\leq t\leq t_0,

and give a,b explicitly in terms of (t_0,\alpha _0,\alpha _1,\beta _0,\beta _1).

(E) Amplitude analysis.

(i) (A universal necessary bound)  
Prove that every admissible steering control must satisfy  

 M \geq  M_low:=max{4|h_1|/(1-e^{-4t_0}), 3|h_2|/(e^{3t_0}-1)}, h=(h_1,h_2).

(ii) (Amplitude of the minimum-energy control)  
For u*(t)=a e^{-4t}+b e^{3t} show that
  (a) u* is strictly monotone when ab \leq  0;  
  (b) when ab > 0 it has a unique stationary point  

    t_c = (1/7) ln(4a/3b)  

   which is a strict local minimum of u* if a,b > 0 and a strict local maximum of u* if a,b < 0.  

Deduce that in every case  

 M_* := ess sup_{t\in [0,t_0]}|u*(t)| = max{|a+b|, |a e^{-4t_0}+b e^{3t_0}|}.

(iii) (Simultaneous budgets)

 (a) Show that if E \geq  E_min(t_0) and M \geq  M_* then the 2n-system (1) is reachable in time t_0 (sufficiency).  

 (b) Show that if either E < E_min(t_0) or M < M_low the transfer is impossible (necessity).  

 (c) Explain why in general M_low \leq  M_*, with strict inequality possible.  
Give an explicit numerical example (specify n,t_0,\alpha _0,\beta _0,\alpha _1,\beta _1) for which M_low < M_*, and construct a steering control that respects the smaller bound M_low (necessarily of higher energy than u*).

(F) Give an explicit initial condition that violates (2) (e.g. x_{10}=1,x_{20}=0) and use the invariants from (A) to prove that no admissible control can make the trajectory reach S in finite time.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
(A) Invariance.  
Differentiate \Delta _{ij}(t)=e^{-4t}(x_i-x_j):

 d\Delta _{ij}/dt = e^{-4t}(4x_i+u-4x_j-u)-4e^{-4t}(x_i-x_j)=0.

The same computation with \Gamma _{ij}(t)=e^{3t}(y_i-y_j) gives d\Gamma _{ij}/dt=0.  
Hence the (2n-2) quantities are conserved for every admissible control u.

(B) Condition (2).  
If X(\tau )\in S then x_i(\tau )=x_j(\tau ) and y_i(\tau )=y_j(\tau ); consequently \Delta _{ij}(\tau )=\Gamma _{ij}(\tau )=0.  
Because the invariants are constant, they must already vanish at t=0, which is exactly (2).

(C) Reduction to two dimensions.  
Under (2) set \alpha :=x_1=\cdots =x_n and \beta :=y_1=\cdots =y_n; substituting into (1) yields (3).  
Solving (3) on [0,t_0] and imposing (\alpha (t_0),\beta (t_0))=(\alpha _1,\beta _1) produces

 Tu = (\int _0^{t_0}e^{-4s}u(s)ds, \int _0^{t_0}e^{3s}u(s)ds)=h,

i.e. the affine subspace (4).

(D) Minimum-energy synthesis.

(i) Let c=(c_1,c_2)\neq 0.  If T* c=0, then c_1e^{-4t}+c_2e^{3t}=0 for all t\in [0,t_0], impossible because the two exponentials are linearly independent. Hence T* is injective and W=T T* is positive definite.

(ii) Because Range T is closed, the orthogonal projection of 0 onto U is u*=T*W^{-1}h; its energy is \langle h,W^{-1}h\rangle .

(iii)  Computations:

 w_{11}=\int _0^{t_0}e^{-8s}ds=(1-e^{-8t_0})/8,  
 w_{22}=\int _0^{t_0}e^{6s}ds=(e^{6t_0}-1)/6,  
 w_{12}=w_{21}=\int _0^{t_0}e^{-s}ds=(1-e^{-t_0}).

Let \Delta  := w_{11}w_{22}-w_{12}^2 > 0.  Then

 a=(w_{22}h_1-w_{12}h_2)/\Delta , b=(-w_{12}h_1+w_{11}h_2)/\Delta ,

so u*(t)=a e^{-4t}+b e^{3t}.

(E) Amplitude questions.

(i)  By Holder

 |h_1| \leq  M\int _0^{t_0}e^{-4s}ds = M(1-e^{-4t_0})/4,  
 |h_2| \leq  M\int _0^{t_0}e^{3s}ds  = M(e^{3t_0}-1)/3,

whence M \geq  M_low.

(ii)  Put f(t)=u*(t)=a e^{-4t}+b e^{3t}.  
 * If ab\leq 0, f'(t)=-4a e^{-4t}+3b e^{3t} keeps a constant sign, so |f| is monotone and attains its maximum at one of the endpoints.  
 * If ab>0, f' vanishes only at  

  t_c=(1/7)ln(4a/3b).  

  Because f''(t)=16a e^{-4t}+9b e^{3t}, we obtain  

   f''(t_c)=28a e^{-4t_c}.  

  Hence t_c is a strict local minimum of f when a,b>0 and a strict local maximum of f when a,b<0.  In either case it is a strict local minimum of |f|, so the essential supremum of |u*| is still attained at an endpoint.  

Consequently  

 M_* = max{|f(0)|,|f(t_0)|} = max{|a+b|, |a e^{-4t_0}+b e^{3t_0}|}.

(iii) Simultaneous budgets.

(a)  If E \geq  E_min and M \geq  M_*, the control u* satisfies both constraints, hence (1) is reachable.

(b)  If E<E_min no element of U has enough energy; if M<M_low no element of U can obey the amplitude bound.  Reachability is impossible.

(c)  The inequality M_low \leq  M_* is immediate because u* itself belongs to U.  Strict inequality can occur.

Example with strict inequality and explicit admissible control.  
Take  

 n = 3, t_0 = 1, \alpha _0 = \beta _0 = 0, \alpha _1 = 1, \beta _1 = \beta ,

where \beta  is chosen so that the two terms in M_low coincide:

 4 e^{-4}\alpha _1 /(1-e^{-4}) = 3 e^{3}\beta  /(e^{3}-1).

Solving gives  

 \beta  = \alpha _1\cdot [4 e^{-4}(e^{3}-1)]/[3 e^{3}(1-e^{-4})] \approx  2.364 \times  10^{-2}.

For these data  

 h = (e^{-4}\alpha _1, e^{3}\beta ) \approx  (1.8315\cdot 10^{-2}, 4.740\cdot 10^{-1}),  

 M_low = 4|h_1|/(1-e^{-4}) \approx  7.45 \times  10^{-2}.  

The minimum-energy control computed above yields  

 M_* \approx  1.23 \times  10^{-1} > M_low.

A steering control that respects the smaller bound is the constant control  

 u_const(t) = M_low\cdot sign(\alpha _1) (here positive), 0 \leq  t \leq  t_0.

Indeed

 Tu_const = ( M_low(1-e^{-4})/4 , M_low(e^{3}-1)/3 ) = h,

so the state reaches the target in time t_0 while |u_const(t)| = M_low for all t.  
Its energy E_const = M_low^2 t_0 is strictly larger than E_min, thereby illustrating the requested phenomenon.

(F)  Take X_0=(1,0,0,\ldots ,0,0,\ldots ,0).  Then \Delta _{12}(0)=1, hence \Delta _{12}(t)\equiv 1 and x_1(t)\neq x_2(t) for all t\geq 0.  Therefore the trajectory can never lie in S, regardless of the control.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension: The problem is posed in 2n dimensions (n≥3), instead of 2 or 3.  
2. Multiple constraints: Both an L∞ (amplitude) and an L² (energy) constraint are enforced simultaneously.  
3. Reduction & functional analysis: Reachability is reduced to an infinite–dimensional moment problem; solving it calls for Hilbert–space projection arguments and computation of a controllability Gramian, techniques absent from the original problem.  
4. Optimisation: The solver must identify and compute the minimum–energy control (quadratic optimisation in function space) and then sharpen it to an amplitude–optimal control, mixing optimal–control theory with real analysis.  
5. Necessity & sufficiency: One has to prove precise reachability conditions (parts (B), (E), (F)), not merely supply a single steering control.  
6. Interaction of concepts: Linear invariants, controllability, Gramian inversion, and optimisation under mixed L²/L∞ constraints all interact, greatly deepening the technical load relative to the original question, which used only a single elementary invariant and elementary integration.
